item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah yes, the pin of shutting the fuck up. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed it! The historical angle gives it enough of a narrative - I didn't know the exact context, but I did know how important orators were in Roman times and when I realized this was basically a political assassination tool I felt well rewarded for piecing it together. Keep writing Roman stuff, you clearly know your history and you're doing well with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, my second SCP, also Roman themed, is done. I hope you like it, and hopefully it doesn't fall into the "magic item" stereotype. Thank you to everyone who reviewed this for your feedback!

+ A Bit of History
- A Bit of History

The "Fulvia" referred to several times in this SCP was an important woman in late-Republican Rome, having been married to the scandalous tribune Clodius, orator and praetor Scribonius, and the famous triumvir Mark Antony. All three supported Julius Caesar and helped to bring about the rise of the Roman Empire. Oligarchical rule was not without its detractors, however - perhaps one of the most famous is the orator Cicero, a former consul and one of the most influential politicians of all time.

On the order of Mark Antony, Cicero was killed and beheaded. When Fulvia received his head, according to some historians she pulled out his tongue and stabbed it repeatedly with her hairpin, a symbolic revenge against his power of speech.

Other SCPs: SCP-3397 - Fortes Fortuna Iuvat!
